# Figure description: скв_394.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_394.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log from a Soviet-era exploratory drilling program. It displays multiple geophysical logging curves recorded along the borehole depth, accompanied by a lithological column and data tables. At the bottom of the sheet there is a separate panel labeled **"контрольные кривые"** (control curves), which is a quality-control check section showing repeated or reference log traces.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure header identifies this as **УЧ-К КАПИТОНОВСКИЙ** (Kapitonovsky site/area, likely a prospecting borehole or section). The log appears to cover a substantial depth interval — based on the length of the log strip and typical Soviet 1:200,000 survey practice, likely on the order of **several hundred metres** (exact depth range is partially illegible due to scan quality, but depth tick marks are visible along the left margin at regular intervals). The borehole number and precise map sheet code are noted in the upper left and right corners but are largely **[illegible]** in this scan. References to **скв** (скважина = borehole) and associated expedition/organization details appear in the header block.

---

## Key Content

The log contains **multiple vertical tracks** arranged left to right:

1. **Leftmost columns**: A **lithological/stratigraphic column** showing rock types through symbolic patterns (dotted patterns suggesting sandy/granular layers, horizontal lines suggesting clayey/shale layers, brick-like patterns suggesting limestone or dense rock). Several distinct lithological units are identifiable by changes in pattern. Formation boundaries are marked with horizontal lines.

2. **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity) curves**: One or more resistivity tracks are present (labeled **КС**). The curves show pronounced **high-amplitude spikes** at several depth intervals — particularly notable in the middle and lower portions of the log — suggesting **dense, resistive layers** (likely carbonate, sandstone, or crystalline rock). The curve labeled **"работа по"** [illegible] is annotated in the upper-middle portion.

3. **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous potential, SP) curves**: A relatively smooth curve running alongside the resistivity track, showing deflections corresponding to permeable/sandy beds versus shale baseline. The SP curve is visible but faint.

4. **ГК or ПГК (gamma or gamma-gamma logging)**: A second set of curves on the right side of the log panel, labeled with what appears to be **ГК** or **ПГК**, showing irregular, higher-frequency variations. Annotations mid-log reference **"Радиоактивный каротаж"** (radioactive logging) — specifically **РК-1** or similar designation, confirming gamma-ray or neutron logging was performed.

5. **Anomaly zones**: Several depth intervals show **strong positive resistivity anomalies** (sharp rightward deflections of the КС curve) — particularly prominent in approximately the upper-third to mid-section of the log. These may correspond to **mineralized, cemented, or carbonate horizons** of exploration interest.

6. **Right-side data tables**: Columns on the far right contain tabular data — likely **core recovery percentages, lithological descriptions, formation names, depth intervals, and possibly assay or permeability data** — but these are largely **[illegible]** due to scan resolution.

7. **Control curves panel** (bottom): The section labeled **"контрольные кривые"** shows abbreviated repeat runs of the КС and possibly SP/gamma curves over a shallower reference interval, used to verify instrument calibration and log repeatability.

## Research Value

This composite well log provides a **continuous record of subsurface physical properties** (electrical resistivity, spontaneous potential, and radioactivity) correlated with a lithological column for the Kapitonovsky borehole in northern Kazakhstan. A geologist would use it to **identify permeable reservoir intervals, resistive (potentially mineralized or carbonate) horizons, and formation boundaries** — key data for assessing the structural and mineral potential of the subsurface at this site. In the context of Report 36572's regional 1:200,000 survey, this log would contribute to **correlating lithological units between boreholes** and constraining the depth and character of geophysical anomalies detected by surface seismic, gravity, and magnetic surveys.
